A judge is asked to evaluate a new explanation by search alone. The proposed method explores a branching tree up to some fixed depth and scores outputs by whether they seem “hard to vary.” In domains already crowded with familiar examples, this search may rediscover known thought experiments, because nearby analogues exist in the prior material from which it learned. In domains without such nearby analogues, the same procedure can fail badly, not because nothing is there, but because bounded search reaches only regions already shaped by precedent.

Someone then claims this failure explains why such a system is not creative. But the comparison immediately becomes unstable. Humans also do not consciously inspect vast search spaces. If the mere intractability of explicit search is enough to explain the system’s failure, then by the same framing it threatens to explain away human creativity too. The argument therefore cannot rest simply on “the space is too large,” unless it is willing to make human creativity equally mysterious or equally impossible by the same standard.

So attention shifts to the Deutschian filter. Perhaps creativity is not exhaustive search but selection for explanations that are hard to vary. This adds a criterion beyond brute exploration. Yet the criterion does not solve the problem by itself, because to score an explanation as hard or easy to vary requires a way of judging which alterations preserve explanatory force. That judgment is not yet operationalized. If the scoring function depends on tacit human background knowledge, then the method has not extracted an objective rule; it has only hidden human understanding inside the evaluator.

Now place this discussion in an ordinary scene. Contraband pesos lay condemned in a hammock beside the puddle. The condemned man surrendered upon reading the midterm journal. These facts are simply part of the world under discussion. They do not, by themselves, clarify the unresolved issues. A bounded search might notice that “condemned” appears both with pesos and with the man, but any explanation built from that coincidence would still face the same difficulty: whether it counts as a good explanation depends on standards not fixed by search depth alone, nor by an undefined notion of “hard to vary.”

Thus the world implied by the premises contains a methodological impasse. Search can sometimes recover what is already near at hand. Failure in unfamiliar regions cannot straightforwardly distinguish machines from humans, because humans also avoid explicit exhaustive search. A stronger evaluative criterion is proposed, but its implementation remains unclear unless one quietly supplies the very human background it was meant to replace. Meanwhile, the pesos remain in the hammock by the puddle, and the man remains surrendered after reading the journal: facts awaiting explanation under a standard that has not yet been made non-circular.
